plan.title: Plugins source pointers and AgentCore harness
plan.summary: Cross-root, diff-backed onboarding walkthrough for OSS PR #554 and enterprise PR #966. Diffs keep stable base refs and intentionally use the current workspace files as the right side, so follow-up review edits show up without repinning every stop to a new commit. Start with the public AgentRegistry contract in OSS: Agent composition, Plugin source pointers, status, validation, storage, controller wiring, source resolution, bundle scanning, translation, materialization, and tests. Then follow the enterprise consumer: AWS deployment adapter, registry/AWS translators, AgentCore harness deploy config, prebuilt Claude Code runner, A2A activation, image build/mirror flow, and smoke/EKS verification path.

steps.title: OSS: Agent composition starts at the public API
steps.explanation: Start here because this is the top-level contract reviewers will see first. The PR puts `plugins`, `skills`, `instructions`, and `mcpServers` directly on `AgentSpec`, so composition is part of the Agent model rather than hidden inside a harness-specific nested block. That makes the later enterprise deploy code resolve one consistent set of refs.

steps.title: OSS: Harness source is a selector, not a bag of refs
steps.explanation: `AgentSource.Harness` identifies the harness type/version while composition remains on `AgentSpec`. This is the important mental model for reviewers: the harness tells deploy code how to run the agent, while top-level refs say what should be materialized into that runtime.

steps.title: OSS: Ref defaults persist before deploy-time lookup
steps.explanation: `ResolveRefs` defaults empty `Kind` values in place for MCP servers, plugins, skills, and instructions. This tiny-looking behavior matters because the enterprise translation path later uses `ref.Kind` to pick the right store; defaulting only in a local copy would pass validation but fail during deployment.

steps.title: OSS: Validation protects the Phase 2 shape
steps.explanation: Validation keeps the current Agent shape unambiguous: harness and image are mutually exclusive, plugin/skill/instruction composition refs require a harness source, and ref kinds must match their fields. MCP server refs are broader than harness; the helper names now use `ResourceRefs` so the distinction is visible in the diff.

steps.title: OSS: Deployment policy carries Claude permission mode
steps.explanation: `DeploymentSpec.HarnessPolicy.PermissionMode` is the bridge from declarative Deployment intent to the Claude runner's runtime behavior. Keep this in mind for the enterprise tour: the adapter extracts this field, the translators carry it, and the runner turns it into the Claude CLI permission-mode flag.

steps.title: OSS: Plugin spec is intent, status is observation
steps.explanation: This is the PR #554 center of gravity. `PluginSpec` is user intent: title, description, harness compatibility, and source pointer. `PluginStatus` is controller-observed state: resolved source, parsed manifest, inventory, conditions, and observed generation. The registry hosts no plugin bytes; it records enough observed state for review and deployment.

steps.title: OSS: ResolvedSource and Inventory are review surfaces
steps.explanation: `ResolvedSource` gives deploy code an immutable pin, and `PluginInventory` gives reviewers a derived risk surface: skills, commands, sub-agents, hooks, MCP servers, and executables. The inventory is intentionally derived from actual files, not just what the plugin manifest claims.

steps.title: OSS: Source validation keeps pins deterministic
steps.explanation: Validation requires a source, enforces git-vs-OCI one-of shape, rejects branch plus commit together, and requires full 40-character commit SHAs when a commit is specified. Branch-only and no-ref git sources are allowed because the controller resolves them to a concrete commit in status.

steps.title: OSS: Manifest parsing is intentionally lossless
steps.explanation: `PluginManifest` is the typed form of `.claude-plugin/plugin.json`, not a new registry kind. The design goal is fidelity: unions keep their original shape, explicit zero values survive, sparse manifests avoid null spam, and unknown top-level keys land in `Extras` so future Claude/Codex fields are preserved.

steps.title: OSS: MCP manifest union stays faithful
steps.explanation: This smaller stop shows the same fidelity principle on the MCP side. `mcpServers` can be a path-like string or an object of server configs, and the parser preserves enough structure to round-trip rather than flattening everything into a lossy registry-only shape.

steps.title: OSS: Plugin enters the generic CRUD surface
steps.explanation: The transport layer stays simple: Plugin is registered with the existing generic CRUD machinery. This is a good review waypoint because it confirms the new kind did not grow a custom handler path just to support source-pointer status.

steps.title: OSS: Store construction now consumes kind descriptors
steps.explanation: `NewStores` no longer owns a second kind-to-table map. It keeps only an OSS built-in allowlist, then takes each built-in kind's table name and storage mode from `v1alpha1.KindDescriptor`. Plugin still becomes a normal tagged-artifact store, but the important review point is broader: registering kind metadata is now the source for store construction too, while extension kinds remain wired explicitly by the composition root.

steps.title: OSS: Migration adds spec/status/generation storage
steps.explanation: The table shape mirrors other tagged resources: spec JSONB, status JSONB, generation, content hash, labels, annotations, and soft-delete fields. The triggers matter for controller behavior because Plugin changes need status notifications and control-plane events like other first-class kinds.

steps.title: OSS: Composition root wires the new controllers
steps.explanation: This is the top-level runtime wiring. The app builds stores, starts the Deployment controller, then starts the Plugin and Skill controllers explicitly. Notice the dependency choice: the Plugin controller gets `pluginsource.NewGitResolver()`, not a registry-hosted bundle store.

steps.title: OSS: Controller contract is source to status
steps.explanation: The Plugin controller has one job: resolve a source pointer, parse manifest and inventory, and write observed state into status. The comments are useful onboarding material because they state the core invariant directly: the registry hosts no plugin bytes.

steps.title: OSS: Kind registration is already partly centralized
steps.explanation: Reviewer aside: the project has a per-kind descriptor registry via each type's `MustRegisterKind[...]` call. That descriptor carries the typed constructor, plural route name, table default, and storage mode, and this tour now shows store construction consuming that metadata directly. The remaining duplication is mostly companion wiring: CRUD has a typed binding map because Go generics need a concrete `T` at `resource.Register[T]`, and controller startup remains behavior-specific because each controller has different dependencies. Future cleanup can still make adding kinds friendlier with a small app-level helper or generated typed bindings, while leaving DB migrations and special controllers explicit.

steps.title: OSS: Queueing is generation-aware and level-triggered
steps.explanation: This stop explains how reconcile work is selected. The controller enqueues unreconciled items and treats `ObservedGeneration` as the done marker. That means a status update for generation N does not mask future spec generation N+1 changes.

steps.title: OSS: Plugin and Skill share controller scaffolding
steps.explanation: Reviewer aside: this is the best candidate for a shared base controller. Plugin and Skill both have the same workqueue lifecycle, wakeup/resync loop, paginated list-and-decode pass, generation gate, missing-row behavior, retryable requeue, terminal `ObservedGeneration` bump, and raw status patch helper. A generic `sourceStatusController[T, Status, Key]` or small unexported helper set could own that scaffolding while each resource keeps its decode, reconciled predicate, status mutation, and source-specific resolver. Deployment should probably stay out of this base: it has durable event replay/checkpoints, finalizers, adapter apply/remove, dependency fingerprints, and discovered-state rules that make it a different controller family.

steps.title: OSS: Reconcile flow writes Ready status from actual source
steps.explanation: Walk this as a pipeline: set Progressing, resolve source, parse manifest, build inventory, then write `Ready=True` with `ResolvedSource`, `Manifest`, and `Inventory`. The status is derived from the checked-out bundle, not copied from spec.

steps.title: OSS: Terminal and retryable errors stay distinct
steps.explanation: Unsupported sources, missing refs, and malformed bundles become terminal-ish status reasons; transient resolver failures are returned so the controller can retry. The raw status patch helper also bumps observed generation only after status is written, which keeps reconciliation honest.

steps.title: OSS: Resolver pins git, loads bundle, classifies failure
steps.explanation: The source resolver turns `Plugin.Spec.Source` into `Plugin.Status.ResolvedSource` plus a canonical bundle. It resolves branch/tag/HEAD to a commit, shallow-clones that exact commit, loads the bundle, and maps unsupported or missing refs to terminal sentinels while leaving network failures retryable.

steps.title: OSS: Git source resolution overlaps with Skill pinning
steps.explanation: Reviewer aside: this is not a totally separate git implementation. The Plugin resolver already delegates low-level git parsing, `ls-remote`, shallow clone, exact commit checkout, timeouts, and ref safety to `internal/cli/common/gitutil`. What is new is Plugin-specific orchestration: source-type dispatch, terminal/retryable classification, loading a canonical bundle, and deriving manifest/inventory. The nearest real overlap is Skill pinning, which also resolves a `v1alpha1.Repository` to an immutable commit via `gitutil.ResolveRefContext` but does not clone or scan content. A follow-up could extract a small shared registry-source helper for `Repository -> commit` plus terminal reason mapping; do not merge Plugin's bundle loading into Skill's lighter resolver.

steps.title: OSS: Git helper bounds clones and resolves refs without cloning
steps.explanation: This shared helper now supports context-bounded git operations, safe ref handling, exact commit checkout, and `git ls-remote` ref resolution. The Plugin resolver leans on this to avoid long-running worker hangs and to store a concrete commit even when users submit a branch.

steps.title: OSS: Canonical bundle is bounded and path-safe
steps.explanation: `CanonicalBundle` is just a path-keyed in-memory file set, but this stop is worth reviewing because it defines the trust boundary. File count and byte totals are capped, symlinks and `.git` are skipped, and every path is normalized and traversal-checked.

steps.title: OSS: Manifest and inventory are derived from the bundle
steps.explanation: `ParseManifest` reads the real `.claude-plugin/plugin.json`; `BuildInventory` scans the file set for actual skills, agents, commands, bin entries, hooks, and MCP servers. This is the legible review surface that status exposes without trusting manifest declarations as the whole truth.

steps.title: Enterprise: Deploy path packs plugin materialization
steps.explanation: This is the live consumer shape in PR #966. The registry translator still passes Ready Plugin/Skill git URLs, subfolders, and resolved commits to the enterprise deployer, but the deployer now materializes those sources before the runner boots. It fetches the pinned source in a temporary checkout, selects the requested subfolder, and packs the resulting filesystem into the runtime config. That keeps OSS focused on source-pointer status and inventory while avoiding a boot-time git dependency in AgentCore.

steps.title: OSS: Reconcile tests prove success, terminal, retryable
steps.explanation: These tests are the fastest way to explain the controller contract. They cover successful status population, unsupported-source terminal status, retryable transient failure, and malformed manifest handling.

steps.title: OSS: Agent harness tests lock the public shape
steps.explanation: These tests are good onboarding guardrails: harness type required, image mutually exclusive, wrong ref kinds rejected, top-level composition requiring a harness, kind defaulting persisted, and `HarnessConfig` remaining selector-only.

steps.title: OSS: Manifest tests prove lossless round-trip behavior
steps.explanation: Close PR #554 with the fidelity tests. They exercise real-ish Claude manifest shapes, extras, explicit zeros, sparse output, and no stray nulls. This gives reviewers confidence that status manifest parsing will not quietly corrupt plugin metadata.

steps.title: Enterprise: Deployment view extracts harness policy
steps.explanation: Now switch to PR #966. The deployment adapter flattens registry objects into a `deploymentView`, including `HarnessPermissionMode`. This is the first consumer of the OSS `DeploymentSpec.HarnessPolicy` field and the handoff point from API intent into deploy orchestration.

steps.title: Enterprise: AWS adapter passes harness images and permission mode
steps.explanation: The AWS runtime config now accepts `harnessImages`, and the adapter forwards both the image map and `HarnessPermissionMode` into the translation request. This is where reviewers should check runtime configurability: the harness runner image is supplied by Runtime config, not hardcoded in the translator.

steps.title: Enterprise: Translation has registry and runtime stages
steps.explanation: This file frames the deploy pipeline. Registry translation resolves AgentRegistry resources into desired runtime state; runtime translation turns that desired state into provider-specific deploy input. The request now carries harness images and permission mode across that boundary.

steps.title: Enterprise: Target stays platform, Route names deploy strategy
steps.explanation: `CloudTarget` remains the provider/runtime target, so a harness deployment on AgentCore still targets `aws_agentcore`. `DeployRoute` is provider-neutral strategy: `source_agent` means build and run from the source bundle, while `harness_agent` means use the prebuilt harness runtime. Empty legacy routes normalize to `source_agent` so older callers do not create a nameless third path.

steps.title: Enterprise: Desired-state model gains Harness
steps.explanation: `AgentDeploySpec.Harness` is the provider-neutral desired state created from OSS Agent composition. It carries the harness image, model, permission mode, plugin sources, skill sources, and instruction prompt so AWS translation can produce an AgentCore harness deploy config.

steps.title: Enterprise: Harness errors and lookup stores are explicit
steps.explanation: Before resolving an Agent, the translator defines typed errors for missing harness image, unsupported harness, and not-ready Plugin/Skill refs. The lookup surface also knows how to fetch Plugin, Skill, and Prompt resources by namespace/name/tag.

steps.title: Enterprise: Registry translation derives harness route
steps.explanation: `TranslateAgentDeploy` keeps `Target` equal to the request platform and records the harness decision in the provider-neutral `Route`. It now carries explicit env and telemetry separately in desired state; platform/runtime translators decide how those become runtime env. That keeps the registry stage from knowing AgentCore's source-agent or harness container contract.

steps.title: Enterprise: resolveAgentHarness gathers model, image, refs, policy
steps.explanation: This is the central enterprise consumer of the OSS API. It currently supports `claude-code`, requires a configured harness image, resolves Plugin/Skill/Prompt refs, and copies model provider/name plus permission mode into desired state.

steps.title: Enterprise: Plugin refs require Ready and resolved git pins
steps.explanation: This is where the OSS Plugin status contract is enforced. A Plugin must be `Ready=True`, have `ResolvedSource`, and currently be a git source with a resolved commit. The deploy path consumes observed pins, not raw branch names from spec.

steps.title: Enterprise: Skills and Prompt instructions follow the same pattern
steps.explanation: Skills are resolved through the same Ready/resolved-source pattern, while instructions resolve from a Prompt ref into text. This stop shows the complete Agent composition path before the provider-specific AWS translator takes over.

steps.title: Enterprise: MCP config remains top-level and deploymentRef-aware
steps.explanation: This larger stop is worth keeping because it answers a common reviewer question: where did MCP go? It stayed on the existing top-level MCP path, including support for MCP deployment refs and remote runtime config, instead of being nested inside harness-specific config.

steps.title: Enterprise: AWS translator branches on route
steps.explanation: Provider-specific translation stays small at the entry point: it normalizes the route, sends `harness_agent` to the harness deploy branch, and keeps `source_agent` on the existing code-deploy path. Source-agent env defaults are applied by the AWS translator, so the shared registry translator does not need to know AgentCore's runtime env contract.

steps.title: Enterprise: AgentCore env defaults live in AWS adapter
steps.explanation: This is the review-feedback fix in one stop. Source-agent AgentCore defaults like `HOST`, `PORT`, `MCP_TERMINATE_ON_CLOSE`, and telemetry env are now built inside the AWS translator package. User env still wins, and harness agents skip this helper because the custom runner owns its own runtime contract.

steps.title: Enterprise: AWS harness plan maps resolved inputs to deploy config
steps.explanation: `translateHarnessAgentDeploy` turns provider-neutral harness state into an AgentCore harness deploy config. It copies the runner image, model, permission mode, env, MCP config, plugin pins, skill pins, and instructions into the shape the deploy client will serialize for the runner.

steps.title: Enterprise: AWS translator test proves the handoff shape
steps.explanation: These tests prove both sides of the env split: resolved harness inputs still become an AgentCore harness plan without source-agent defaults, while source-agent plans receive the AgentCore runtime env and telemetry defaults inside the AWS adapter.

steps.title: Enterprise: Empty route normalizes to source_agent
steps.explanation: This small compatibility test prevents an empty route from leaking through as an implicit third strategy. Older callers can still omit the route, but provider translation emits the explicit `source_agent` route in the plan.

steps.title: Enterprise: Deploy config becomes runner runtime JSON
steps.explanation: `AgentCoreHarnessDeployConfig` is the in-process deploy shape, while `HarnessRuntimeConfig` is what the prebuilt runner reads from an env var. This is a clean boundary: deployment owns cloud/runtime configuration; the runner owns activation.

steps.title: Enterprise: AgentCore deploy serializes harness config into env
steps.explanation: `DeployHarnessToAgentCore` builds the runtime config, serializes it into the harness env var, carries network/OAuth settings, and calls the AgentCore client with a prebuilt container image. This is where the runner receives everything it needs at startup.

steps.title: Enterprise: Client deploys a prebuilt container runtime
steps.explanation: `DeployHarness` validates the request, derives resource names, confirms the ECR repository ARN for the harness image, and launches AgentCore with an HTTP server protocol and container artifact. This is different from the Python code-deploy artifact path.

steps.title: Enterprise: IAM grants ECR pull only for harness images
steps.explanation: The execution policy keeps existing AgentCore permissions and conditionally adds ECR pull grants only when a harness container image is involved. The image parser also requires same-account, same-region ECR, which aligns with the smoke docs and avoids deploying arbitrary external images.

steps.title: Enterprise: Discovery redacts secret-shaped metadata
steps.explanation: This support change keeps discovery/status output safer by recursively redacting sensitive-looking keys. It is adjacent to the harness work because runtime metadata and env-heavy deploy flows make accidental secret exposure easier.

steps.title: Enterprise: Runner startup activates config in order
steps.explanation: The runner starts by loading runtime config, installing plugins, installing skills, writing MCP config, setting provider env, configuring Claude CLI args, and serving AgentCore requests. This is the activation sequence the smoke test is proving.

steps.title: Enterprise: Runner image bundles Go runner and Claude Code
steps.explanation: The Dockerfile builds the Go runner, installs Node and Claude Code, copies the binary, creates a non-root `claude` user, and sets `HOME`/`CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR`. This is the artifact AgentCore runs instead of a generated Python source bundle.

steps.title: Enterprise: Runner unpacks prepared plugins and skills
steps.explanation: The runner no longer shells out to git. It receives packed file payloads in `AGENTREGISTRY_HARNESS_CONFIG`, unpacks each plugin or skill into a safe directory under `CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR`, and returns plugin directories for Claude Code `--plugin-dir` activation. The only boot-time dependency is the prepared env payload.

steps.title: Enterprise: Env bundle is compressed and path-safe
steps.explanation: This is the temporary transport. The deployer serializes plugin/skill files as JSON, gzip-compresses that JSON, and base64-encodes it for the harness env config. The unpacker rejects absolute paths, traversal, backslashes, bad encodings, and targets outside the destination; the packer skips `.git`, symlinks, and non-regular files. This is intentionally an interim bridge until a real artifact-distribution path replaces the env-var payload.

steps.title: Enterprise: Runner writes strict Claude MCP config
steps.explanation: MCP server config is written into a temporary Claude-compatible config file and later passed with `--strict-mcp-config`. This ties the top-level OSS MCP refs to actual Claude Code invocation behavior.

steps.title: Enterprise: Claude CLI args carry plugins, MCP, model, permissions
steps.explanation: The runner builds Claude CLI args from runtime config: plugin dirs, strict MCP config, model, and permission mode. The default permission mode is intentionally permissive for this harness path unless deployment policy overrides it.

steps.title: Enterprise: Runner exposes ping, direct invoke, and A2A paths
steps.explanation: The HTTP server implements `/ping` plus AgentCore invocation handling. It supports direct text prompts and A2A-shaped message bodies, then prefixes configured instructions before calling Claude. This is why the smoke can prove both activation and runtime invocation.

steps.title: Enterprise: A2A helpers shape stream and task responses
steps.explanation: A2A parsing extracts text from message bodies, and response helpers build completed stream events or JSON task responses. This is the compatibility bridge that let the live smoke talk to AgentCore's A2A-ish invocation contract.

steps.title: Enterprise: Activation tests lock CLI and MCP behavior
steps.explanation: These tests prove the runner composes Claude CLI args correctly and writes the expected MCP config. They are the unit-test version of the runtime activation story.

steps.title: Enterprise: Server tests cover A2A stream/send and safe names
steps.explanation: The server tests exercise A2A streaming events, message/send task responses, and safe plugin directory naming. This is the best quick evidence that the runner API contract is not just hand-tested.

steps.title: Enterprise: Build and mirror flow matches AgentCore image rules
steps.explanation: The Makefile adds an arm64 runner build target and a mirror target for copying a published image into a reviewer/demo account's ECR. That matches the client-side same-account ECR validation and keeps PR CI build-only unless publishing is explicitly requested.

steps.title: Enterprise: CI verifies the runner image builds
steps.explanation: The workflow builds the arm64 runner image on relevant PR changes without pushing it. This is an important review stop because image publishing/signing is still a follow-up, but buildability is covered in CI.

steps.title: Enterprise: Runner image docs explain reviewer setup
steps.explanation: This doc is the human-facing companion to the Makefile and IAM/client checks. It tells reviewers why AgentCore requires a private same-account ECR image, how to mirror the standardized runner, and which env var drives the harness smoke.

steps.title: Enterprise: Smoke tool deploys and invokes the harness
steps.explanation: The direct smoke is the latest walkthrough proof for the new harness behavior. It registers the runtime/agent/plugin/deployment shape, deploys to AgentCore, then invokes the runner looking for the expected harness activation response.

steps.title: Enterprise: Smoke flags cover plugins, skills, MCP, env
steps.explanation: This stop shows the breadth of the smoke harness inputs. It can parse plugin specs, skill specs, MCP specs, env vars, model/provider options, and image/runtime settings, so reviewers can reason about future walkthrough variants without reading only the happy path.

steps.title: Enterprise: Smoke invocation uses retry and SigV4
steps.explanation: The invocation code waits for runtime readiness, signs AgentCore requests, and verifies response text. This is the code-level answer to whether the latest added harness path has an executable walkthrough proof, separate from older full EKS walkthrough wiring.

steps.title: Enterprise: EKS walkthrough still wraps the harness smoke
steps.explanation: End the tour by contrasting direct smoke with the EKS walkthrough wrapper. The current full walkthrough path still calls `harness_smoke`, but the newest Phase 2 proof lives in the direct AgentCore smoke tool and runner activation path you just reviewed.
